source_display: AI as Brain Crutches for ADHD
evidence: Vrijen shared his personal interest in building AI tools as 'brain crutches' for ADHD—filling gaps between therapy, medication, and coaching
Vrijen warned against the 'death by a thousand cuts' of endless unpaid pilots. Instead, seek paid pilots with scoped changes and identify design partners willing to work with the product as-is

source_display: PMF as Obscenity Test (Know It When You See It)
evidence: He defined product-market fit using the Supreme Court's obscenity standard: 'you know it when you see it'—when you have it, you don't think about it; when you don't, you obsess over it constantly
Chris revealed he's been building his edtech product for almost a year, leveraging AI despite being a former special education teacher rather than a developer... an ADHD support product called '360'
Vrijen invited Chris to join the Future of Higher Education group (6,000 members globally, ~1,000 in NYC, ~1,000 in Boston), where Vrijen serves as NYC co-lead
The core concept is a 'marketplace of all possible futures' - allowing users to 'jump into' career simulations mapped to real jobs and companies
Max used the metaphor of a 'neopet' - anytime you train or deploy your AI requires 'energy,' which is where they monetize through usage-based pricing
Aim to sell Careerspan towards the middle of 2026, while using the next few months to pursue revenue generation opportunities to improve valuation
He argued these roles are 'done' in the current AI-enabled landscape, with many PM positions facing similar elimination unless they manage P&L and have product experience
Visible links between individual work and the product/service being sold are increasingly essential in 2026... engineers more than one step removed from the product were most vulnerable
He criticized tools like HackerRank for not grounding results in actual business value - passing an algorithm assessment doesn't prove someone can add value to the company
He introduced context engineering as the foundational practice: not prompting the AI directly, but cultivating the context it uses to generate desired outputs
Three-level framework: Conversation (tactical), Environment (persistent context), Pipeline (external data integration) - mastering all three creates an emergent personal AI operating system

source_display: AI Productivity as Mental Model Problem
evidence: Vrijen argued the core issue isn't a technology or tactics problem but a mental model problem—users collect techniques without understanding how they integrate into effective systems
Threshold rubrics: Ask the AI to create an evaluation rubric, fill it out, assign scores, and iterate output until achieving the highest level
embrace the 'unhobbling' concept from the Anthropic/Claude team—recognizing the AI is capable of more than you initially realize
Network mismatch: Only 3,300 LinkedIn connections versus 45,000 imported contacts from a 2015 data import, revealing two largely disjoint professional networks

source_display: Plan Files as Future of Coding
evidence: Plan files represent coding's future: Mixed prose and code structures for system planning will likely supplant pure coding approaches

three presenters: Vrijen Attawar (CEO and co-founder of Careerspan), Logan Currie (Careerspan co-founder and AI productivity nerd), and Ben Guo (co-founder of Zo Computer and Vrijen as a power Zo user)
Posting correlation: Documented 1,800% increase in inbound connections when increasing posting frequency, strong evidence for visibility strategies
